schools.summary: Paul addresses those who know the law, appealing to the maxim that the law has dominion over a person only as long as he lives — death dissolving the law's claim, a universal legal principle to be applied to the believer's relation to the law (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): the purpose — to show by the marriage analogy that the believer, having died with Christ, is freed from the law's dominion.

themes: the law hath dominion over a man as long as he liveth
I speak to them that know the law
the law binds only the living
the foundation for the believer's release

schools.summary: The married woman is bound by law to her husband while he lives, but his death frees her from the marriage law (Barnes, Robertson) — an illustration of the principle (v.1) that law binds only during life.
Calvin (with Theodoret): the application — the believer, like the woman, is freed by a death to be joined to another, Christ.

themes: the woman is bound by the law to her husband so long as he liveth
if the husband be dead, she is loosed
the marriage analogy
death dissolving the legal bond

schools.summary: Married to another while the husband lives, she is an adulteress; but if he is dead, she is free and no adulteress in marrying another (Barnes, Robertson) — the death of the husband alone dissolving the bond and removing the charge.
Theodoret: only death frees from the marriage law, as only death (with Christ) frees the believer from the law to be joined to Christ.

themes: married to another, she shall be called an adulteress
if her husband be dead, she is free from that law
the completed marriage analogy
death alone frees to a new union

schools.summary: The believer has become dead to the law through the body (the crucified death) of Christ — by union with his death, died to the law's claim — that he might be married to the risen Christ, a new union as the widow to a new husband, to bring forth fruit unto God (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the purpose — this union with Christ is fruitful, producing holiness and good works unto God, the fruit of the new marriage.

themes: ye also are become dead to the law by the body of Christ
married to another, even to him who is raised from the dead
dead to the law, married to Christ
fruit unto God from the new union

schools.summary: When 'in the flesh' (the unrenewed, carnal state before conversion), the sinful passions (the motions of sins), stirred up or occasioned by the law (which by forbidding excites the rebellious heart), worked in the members to bring forth fruit unto death (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): this old state bore fruit unto death — the opposite of the fruit unto God (v.4); the law provoking sin in the carnal heart.

themes: when we were in the flesh, the motions of sins, which were by the law
did work in our members to bring forth fruit unto death
the former state in the flesh
sin stirred by the law unto death

schools.summary: The believer is now delivered (released, discharged) from the law, having died to that which held him in bondage (the bond dissolved by death), that he should serve in newness of spirit (inward, willing, living obedience by the Spirit) and not in the oldness of the letter (the external, dead bondage of the law, Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Theodoret): the new service — the believer serves God in the newness of the Spirit, living and willing obedience, rather than the oldness of the letter, the gospel transforming service from law to Spirit.

themes: now we are delivered from the law
serve in newness of spirit, and not in the oldness of the letter
deliverance from the law unto new service
the Spirit, not the letter

schools.summary: To the cavil 'is the law sin?' (the natural objection from vv.5-6), Paul replies 'God forbid' — the law's office is to reveal sin: 'I had not known sin but by the law,' knowing lust to be sin only because the law said 'Thou shalt not covet' (Barnes, Henry, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): the tenth commandment (against coveting) reaches the heart, revealing inward sin; the law's office is to disclose sin, not to cause it — far from being sin, it is the mirror of sin.

themes: is the law sin? God forbid
I had not known sin, but by the law
the vindication of the law
the law reveals, not produces, sin

schools.summary: Sin (the indwelling principle) took occasion by the commandment (a starting-point, a base of operations) to provoke all manner of concupiscence (evil desire) — the forbidding of a thing exciting the corrupt heart to crave it; and without the law sin was dead, comparatively dormant and unrecognized (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): apart from the law sin lay dormant (dead, inactive, unfelt) — not non-existent but unroused; the law provoking the perversity of the corrupt heart.

themes: sin, taking occasion by the commandment, wrought all manner of concupiscence
without the law sin was dead
sin's use of the commandment
the law provoking the corrupt heart

schools.summary: 'I was alive without the law once' refers to a state of false security (Paul's earlier life, or before the law came home to the conscience) — self-complacent, unawakened; but when the commandment came home in its spiritual force, sin revived (sprang to life, was felt) and the self died, the false security shattered (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the law, coming in power, slays self-righteous complacency, revealing sin and bringing the sentence of death to the conscience.

themes: I was alive without the law once
when the commandment came, sin revived, and I died
the coming of the commandment
the law awakening and slaying the complacent self

schools.summary: The commandment was ordained (designed) to give life ('do this and live'), yet through sin it proved to be unto death — not by its own fault but through human corruption; the law that promised life to the obedient became, to the sinner, an instrument of death (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the cause is not the law's fault but sin's — the good law, meeting a corrupt heart, issues in death.

themes: the commandment, which was ordained to life, I found to be unto death
the commandment's unintended effect
the law death-dealing to the sinner
the fault in sin, not the law

schools.summary: Sin again took occasion by the commandment (as in v.8) to deceive (beguile, as the serpent in Eden, promising pleasure but leading to ruin) and by it slay the sinner — bringing condemnation and death (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): sin, by means of the commandment, slew the sinner — the Edenic pattern (Genesis 3) of sin's deceit working through the good commandment.

themes: sin, taking occasion by the commandment, deceived me, and by it slew me
sin's deceit and slaying
the deadly cunning of indwelling sin
the Edenic pattern of deceit

schools.summary: Paul concludes (the fault being in sin, not the law) that the law and the commandment are holy (pure, from a holy God), just (in their requirements), and good (in their design and tendency) — wholly excellent (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the full acquittal — whatever death the law occasioned is sin's doing, not the law's, which remains holy, just, and good.

themes: the law is holy, and the commandment holy, and just, and good
the vindication of the law
the law's complete excellence
holy, just, and good

schools.summary: To the objection whether the good law became death, Paul replies 'God forbid' — it was sin, not the law, that wrought death; sin is unmasked, shown in its true colors, by working death through the good law, that sin might appear exceeding sinful (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the purpose — that sin, by perverting the good commandment unto death, might appear exceeding sinful (sinful beyond measure); the law's effect exposing the enormity of sin.

themes: was then that which is good made death unto me? God forbid
that sin by the commandment might become exceeding sinful
sin unmasked as exceeding sinful
the law exposing sin's enormity

schools.summary: The law is spiritual (from the Spirit, reaching the spirit) while the 'I' is carnal, sold under sin — opening the great debate over who the 'I' is; Henry frames the controversy as between the convinced-but-unregenerate soul and the renewed-but-imperfect believer.

Calvin, Gill (with Augustine's mature view): the passage describes the regenerate believer — the normal Christian struggle with indwelling sin, even the most advanced beating down all glory of the flesh; the 'I' is Paul as a Christian.

Chrysostom: the law is spiritual, a teacher of virtue; the fault lies in the carnal disciple, not the holy law — tending (with the Arminian reading) to see the unregenerate man or man under the law, not yet the believer aided by the Spirit.

debates.question: Is the 'I' of Romans 7:14-25 the regenerate believer or the unregenerate man?

sides.position: The regenerate believer — the normal Christian struggle with indwelling sin
The unregenerate man under the law, or Paul before conversion — not the normal Christian state

themes: the law is spiritual: but I am carnal, sold under sin
the spiritual law and the carnal self
the great debate over the I
regenerate or unregenerate

schools.summary: The inward division of the will: the 'I' does what it does not approve (allow), wills good but does it not, hates evil yet does it — a war between will and deed, the painful experience of inward division (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (regenerate view): the believer grieving over indwelling sin's resistance to the renewed will — the deed falling short of the holy desire.

themes: that which I do I allow not
what I would, that do I not; but what I hate, that do I
the inward division of the will
the war between will and deed

schools.summary: In doing what the 'I' would not (disapproving its own act), the self consents to the law that it is good — the very reluctance proving agreement with the law's standard, the conscience and the law siding against the deed (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the struggle itself testifies to the law's goodness and the better self's assent to it, confirming the law's goodness (v.12).

themes: if then I do that which I would not, I consent unto the law that it is good
the will's consent to the law
the conflict vindicating the law
the better self siding with the law

schools.summary: Paul distinguishes the true self (the renewed will, or the better judgment) from the sin that does the evil — 'not I, but sin that dwelleth in me,' the remaining principle of corruption residing in the person, locating the evil in the indwelling enemy (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (regenerate view): the believer disowning the sin that wars against his renewed self — the evil traced to indwelling sin, not the true self renewed by grace.

themes: no more I that do it, but sin that dwelleth in me
the locating of sin in indwelling sin
the distinction between the self and sin
indwelling corruption the source

schools.summary: Paul confesses that in his flesh (the unrenewed nature) dwells no good thing — total depravity of the flesh — and that the will to good is present but the power to perform it is lacking, the will outrunning the ability (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (regenerate view): the believer with a renewed will yet unable, through the remaining flesh, to perform the good perfectly — no good in the flesh, the desire outrunning the power.

themes: in me (that is, in my flesh) dwelleth no good thing
to will is present with me; but how to perform I find not
the helplessness of the flesh
the will present, the power lacking

schools.summary: Paul restates the painful contradiction (v.15) for emphasis — the good willed is not done, the evil not willed is done, the gap between desire and performance, a helpless struggle (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (regenerate view): the believer's grief at falling short of his renewed desire — willing good yet doing evil through the remaining flesh.

themes: the good that I would I do not: but the evil which I would not, that I do
the restated inward conflict
the war between will and deed
good willed not done, evil done

schools.summary: Paul again concludes (as v.17) that the unwilled evil is done not by the true self but by indwelling sin — the doing of the unwilled evil proving an alien power at work, distinct from the willing self (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (regenerate view): the believer disowning the indwelling sin that does the evil against his renewed will.

themes: if I do that I would not, it is no more I that do it, but sin that dwelleth in me
the restated locating of sin
the distinction between self and indwelling sin
an alien power at work

schools.summary: Paul finds a 'law' — a fixed principle or rule operating within (not the Mosaic law here) — that when he would do good, evil (indwelling sin) is present, ever at hand to oppose, a settled recurring experience (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Theodoret): the abiding law of the conflict between the renewed will and the remaining corruption — evil ever present when good is willed.

themes: I find then a law, that, when I would do good, evil is present with me
the law of inward conflict
evil present when good is willed
the abiding principle of the struggle

schools.summary: The 'I' delights in (loves, heartily approves) the law of God after the inward man (the renewed mind, the higher self) — a positive, hearty assent, not mere reluctant agreement (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin, Gill: genuine delight in the law of God after the inward man is the mark of the regenerate — only the renewed heart truly delights in God's law (Psalm 1); strong evidence for the believer reading.
Clarke: even an unregenerate, conscientious Jew may approve and delight in the law (Isaiah 58:2) — so this delight does not by itself prove the speaker regenerate.

debates.question: Does 'delight in the law of God after the inward man' prove the speaker is regenerate?

sides.position: Yes — genuine delight in God's law is the mark of the renewed heart (Psalm 1)
No — even a conscientious unregenerate man may approve and delight in the law (Isaiah 58:2)

themes: I delight in the law of God after the inward man
the delight in God's law
the mark of the inward man
the sharpened debate on the I

schools.summary: There is another law (principle) in the members (the flesh) — the law of sin — warring against the law of the mind (the renewed reason that delights in God's law, v.22), bringing the self into captivity to the law of sin (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin, Wesley: the captivity — whether the believer's painful experience of remaining sin's power (regenerate view) or the convicted sinner's bondage; the law of sin taking the self prisoner.

themes: another law in my members, warring against the law of my mind
bringing me into captivity to the law of sin
the two laws at war
captivity to the law of sin

schools.summary: The heart-rending cry of the wretched man ('from the depths of despair,' Sanday and Headlam) — the climax of the conflict, longing for deliverance from 'the body of this death' (the body of sin and death, possibly alluding to the tyrant's punishment of chaining a living man to a corpse, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson).
Calvin (regenerate view): the believer bewailing his unhappy condition and panting for full deliverance while submitting to God's will — not despairing like the profane, but groaning for the remedy.
Clarke: the convicted sinner (or pre-conversion Paul, the three days at Damascus) at the point of despair, just before deliverance is proclaimed through Christ.

themes: O wretched man that I am
who shall deliver me from the body of this death
the cry of the wretched man
longing for deliverance

schools.summary: The cry (v.24) is answered: deliverance comes through Jesus Christ — thanksgiving to God for the rescue the law could not give (some manuscripts read 'the grace of God'); Paul sums up the conflict — the same self serving God's law with the mind but the law of sin with the flesh (Barnes, Robertson).

Clarke: the deliverance (ch. 8) shows the normal Christian state is victory through Christ, so ch. 7 describes the pre-deliverance state of the convicted soul or pre-conversion Paul — we must not lower Christianity to a state of continual defeat.

Calvin, Gill: the believer continues this divided service and conflict through life, the deliverance being begun now by grace and consummated at last; the regenerate man serving God with the mind while the flesh wars on.

debates.question: Does the divided service describe the normal lifelong Christian state or the pre-deliverance state?

sides.position: The believer's lifelong conflict — deliverance begun now, consummated at last
The pre-deliverance state — the normal Christian life (ch. 8) is victory through Christ

themes: I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord
with the mind I serve the law of God; but with the flesh the law of sin
deliverance through Christ
the summary of the divided service
